blog/bifurcation-and-inter-contract-enfo.$value:
The cost of authoring a bounded
abstraction is falling toward zero, so the cost moves somewhere else

The industry hand-maintains
the same command four times — CLI, MCP tool, OpenAPI route, tool schema — and
hopes they stay in sync

What it can't do yet, what nobody has really
solved, is keep all of those honest with each other while it keeps editing them

Hand an agent a crisp contract and it fills in the
implementation, the tests, the docs

The filesystem
door assumes one ownership model; the process door drifts to another; the CLI
says one thing and its MCP adapter says a slightly older thing

A contract no tool enforces is
not a standard; it's a wish, and an agent regresses it the first time it's
convenient

The door checks an owner and a policy,
acts or refuses, and records either way:

It's the problem this whole
 project is aimed at
What's a result and what's still a bet
I grade my own claims against the running code, so plainly:

When agents write most of the software, the scarce resource is trust between
the pieces, not authoring

That
boundary is exactly what makes an agent useful on it

- Enforced today — the drift gates (docs from source) and executable behaviour
 specs

If they break, the build breaks.
- Partial — "privileged effects attributable to a signed owner." True on
 the common path; the named gap is a permission boundary I'm still closing.
- The bet — contracts staying honest between components as they evolve
One sanctioned door per kind of power

They don't. prx derives them from one spec, so the
surfaces can't disagree: there's only one thing to disagree with

The interesting
failure mode is the seam
Same door, one allow, one deny, both recorded

The process spawner, the tool
adapters, the CLI — all of it

Each piece is
internally fine
In agent-built software, the open problem is the seams between the pieces
That gap is the whole problem, and it's where I work
prx is my answer: an argument I make in running code

That's the
bet, and I'm working it in the open, graded against the code
Drift is a build failure, not a code review

The unsolved problem is keeping many bounded, agent-authored abstractions
honest against each other as they evolve — enforcing contracts between ideas,
not just within one

Agentic development works by bifurcation: a good idea earns its own bounded
abstraction with a defined contract. "Filesystem access" becomes a thing with
edges — a name, a surface, a promise about what it does and doesn't do
Authoring is getting cheap

That
 one is stated as direction, not a finished result

So docs generate from source and fail CI on drift, and behaviour
specs execute against the engine
An agent can write the filesystem layer now
The seam is where that trust is won or lost

The capability-seam idea converges with established object-capability and
information-flow research; that mapping is a later post

Trust is mechanical — gates on every change,
not reviewer vigilance that can't scale to agent throughput
The bet: three mechanisms for the seams

The seam is the only path, so the
seam is where authority is decided
The unsolved part is between the abstractions, not inside them

For each ambient capability there is
exactly one way through — a door you hold a socket to, never the keys behind it:
fs, proc, env, gh, git, cas
Most tooling secures a single action

You can scope an agent to a
contract; you can't scope one to a vibe
One source of truth, projected to many surfaces

When authoring is cheap, you get more of it: more seams, more contracts, more
places two agent-authored pieces can quietly come to disagree

blog/verbspec-author-once-project-everyw.$value:
Ship one capability to both humans and agents today and you write it four times:
a CLI with flag parsing and --help, an MCP tool definition, an OpenAPI
operation, an Anthropic tool-use schema

The surfaces aren't generated-then-edited; they're computed from the spec on
demand

If you maintain a CLI and an MCP server and an OpenAPI spec for the same
operations, this collapses four hand-maintained artifacts into one — and the one
can't lie to the other three.

verbspec is the projection layer — author once, project everywhere,
drift-free

A verb's input and output are Zod schemas — the single
source of truth, giving you runtime validation and static types from one
definition. z.toJSONSchema turns it into the interchange IR, and every surface
is a pure function of that IR:
Get the CLI, the MCP tool, the OpenAPI route, and the model schema for free

The help text says one thing, the
tool schema says a slightly older thing, the OpenAPI spec a third

That's
the whole trick: with a single definition, drift has nothing to occur between
You author the verb once:

This is one mechanism from a larger bet — keeping agent-authored contracts
honest as they multiply (see the door is the unit of bounded
authority). verbspec is the
piece with the most pull on its own.

The
capability/security story is separate; that's the door work. verbspec is
MIT, on npm, with zod the only peer dependency

Four artifacts, four hand-maintained
copies of the same shape — and they drift

The package is a clean leaf: its only production dependency is the zod peer
dep, and an extractability test enforces that it stays pure — outward-only
imports, no ambient authority (no shelling out, no process.env)

It handles one job: the "one source of truth projected to many
surfaces" mechanism, pulled out as a standalone, usable thing

Each rots on
its own schedule, and nothing tells you when
There's nothing to keep in sync because there's only one thing

…and the CLI (greet Ada --loud), the MCP tool, the model schema, and
POST /greet are all just that spec seen from different sides

The fix is boring and total: there should be one definition, and every
surface should be a pure projection of it

You can drop
it into anything
One spec, four surfaces

No codegen, no
build step, no FFI to author a verb

blog/why-i-built-this-agent-kept-doing-t.$value:
Six months ago the project was dev-contracts — I wanted to give an agent a
contract it couldn't drift away from

It writes code, runs
commands, and opens pull requests

A solo project, worked out in the open and graded against
the running code — but the first user it has to convince is me

It edits a file I didn't mean to touch, runs a
command I wouldn't have run, or quietly does three extra things on the way to the
one I actually asked for

It
scopes what the agent can reach: the job gets exactly the capabilities it needs
and nothing else
Why I built this: my coding agent kept doing things I didn't ask for

This post is the plain version of
what these tools are and how I got here — usage first, because that's the part
you can't see from the repos

It became prx when the interesting part
turned out to be driving whole units of work, not holding a single contract

When something comes out wrong, I can see which step it came
from.

If a tool here
doesn't earn its keep in my own daily work, it doesn't belong in the project
Same problem, three names

That sentence is the whole project, and until now it was nowhere on
the page
I run an AI coding agent on my own work every day

And
prx grew guest-room when I needed the agent's authority to be the thing
under contract: one sanctioned way to reach each kind of power, instead of
trusting the agent to stay in its lane
Each one is the last one seen more clearly
guest-room is the part I built most recently, and the one I reach for now

If you're chewing on the same thing — an agent that's useful but won't stay in
bounds — I'd like to talk.

That's what keeps the agent from wandering — it can't get into
what it was never handed
Bounded Systems is what I built to stop that
Two things, used together.
None of this showed up as a plan
That loop is the point

The short version, the one that actually lands when I say it out loud:
guest-room is what I did so the agent didn't get distracted or do things I
didn't want

I build these because they help me build these. prx and guest-room are the tools
I use to work on prx and guest-room — every project is meant to make the next one
safer and faster to do with an agent

It's all derivations of one need, renamed each
time I understood it a little better

It earns its place — and it has a habit I got
tired of: left alone, it wanders
Why I keep building it

I hand it an issue ticket and it chains the pull
requests from there, writing structured output at each step — so the path from
"here's the task" to "here's the PR" is a sequence of artifacts I can check, not
one opaque jump

It's also the honest answer to "is this a real thing or an idea?" It's real
because I depend on it
